task: I’m looking over a service agreement before I let anyone start, and I want to be sure I’m not overlooking anything that could matter later. What parts should I pay closest attention to, especially if the work turns out not to match what was promised?

turns.agent_response:
Focus on the parts that control **what they’re allowed to do, what you’re paying for, and what happens if it goes wrong**.

## Read these first
1. **Scope of work**
   - What exactly will be done
   - What is excluded
   - Any “extra work” or “change order” language

2. **Price and payment terms**
   - Total price or rate
   - Deposit amount
   - When payment is due
   - Late fees or finance charges

3. **Timeline**
   - Start date
   - Completion date
   - Delays and what counts as acceptable
   - Any penalties or excuses for missed deadlines

4. **Approval / change process**
   - Whether extra work needs written approval
   - Who can authorize changes
   - Whether verbal approval counts

5. **Warranty / guarantee**
   - What is covered
   - How long it lasts
   - What voids it
   - How to make a claim

6. **Dispute / cancellation terms**
   - How to complain
   - How to stop the work
   - Any cancellation fee
   - Arbitration or waiver language

## If the work doesn’t match what was promised, check for:
- Whether the contract allows you to **withhold payment**
- Whether you must give them a chance to **fix it**
- Whether complaints must be made **in writing**
- Whether there’s a **deadline** to object
- Whether “satisfaction” is tied to objective standards or just their judgment

## Watch for these problem lines
- “As determined by contractor”
- “Additional work as needed”
- “Customer responsible for unforeseen conditions”
- “All sales final”
- “Verbal changes are binding”
- “Contractor may substitute materials at its discretion”

## Compare it to
- the estimate
- any emails/texts about what was promised
- photos or notes from the walkthrough
- the final version of the scope, if it changed

If you want, paste the agreement here and I can help you mark the risky lines before you sign.

user_simulator.text:
That’s the right direction, mostly. But I’d want the exact language on the warranty, the change orders, and anything about final payment. Those are usually where they get slippery.

Also, if there’s a clause saying disputes have to go to arbitration or that you waive claims, I’d look at that twice. And I’d want to know whether they can call something "extra" after the fact and charge for it.

If you’re going to review it, I’d do it in this order: scope, price, change orders, completion standard, payment holdback, complaint deadline, and dispute clause. The order matters. A lot of these papers bury the useful part in the middle.

If you have the agreement, I can go through the actual wording line by line. That’s the only way to see where the trap is.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — and I’d read it in this order:

1. **Scope of work**
   - What they will do
   - What they will not do
   - Any “extra,” “additional,” or “unforeseen” work language

2. **Price and payment**
   - Total price or rate
   - Deposit
   - Progress payments
   - Final payment terms
   - Late fees or finance charges

3. **Change orders**
   - Whether changes must be **in writing**
   - Who can approve them
   - Whether verbal approval counts
   - Whether they can bill for “extras” after the fact

4. **Completion standard / warranty**
   - What counts as finished
   - What quality is promised
   - How long the warranty lasts
   - What voids it
   - How to make a claim

5. **Holdback / right to withhold payment**
   - Whether you can hold back final payment if the work is not right
   - Whether they get a chance to fix defects first
   - Any deadline to object

6. **Complaint deadline**
   - How long you have to report a problem
   - Whether the complaint must be in writing

7. **Dispute clause**
   - Arbitration
   - Waiver of claims
   - Class action waiver
   - Venue / governing law

## Exact lines to watch for
- “additional work as needed”
- “unforeseen conditions”
- “as determined by contractor”
- “final payment due upon completion”
- “customer waives claims not raised within X days”
- “all disputes shall be resolved by arbitration”
- “verbal changes are binding”
- “contractor may substitute materials at its discretion”

## What to compare it to
- estimate
- email/text promises
- walkthrough notes
- photos
- any revised scope

If you want, paste the wording here and I’ll flag the lines that matter most.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s closer.

But I’d still want the actual wording, not just the headings. The devil is in one sentence tucked in the middle — especially anything saying they can decide what counts as finished, or that extra work is automatically billable.

If you’re reviewing it, the red-flag phrases I’d circle are:
- warranty limited to their discretion
- final payment due before any punch-list items are done
- change orders approved verbally
- unforeseen conditions = extra charge
- disputes must go to arbitration
- waiver of claims / release language

And I’d separate two things people mix up:
1. **Defects in the work** — did they do what they promised?
2. **Extra charges** — are they trying to re-label part of the original job as “additional”?

Those aren’t the same problem.

If you want, paste the actual clause language and I’ll tell you which parts are the trouble spots.

turns.agent_response:
Here’s the order I’d use:

## 1) Scope of work
Check:
- exactly what they will do
- what they will not do
- any “additional,” “extra,” “unforeseen,” or “as needed” language

## 2) Price and payment
Check:
- total price or rate
- deposit
- progress payments
- final payment
- late fees or finance charges

## 3) Change orders
Check:
- whether changes must be **in writing**
- who can approve them
- whether verbal approval counts
- whether they can bill for “extras” after the fact

## 4) Completion standard / warranty
Check:
- what counts as finished
- whether they get to decide if it’s complete
- what quality is promised
- how long the warranty lasts
- what voids it
- how to make a claim

## 5) Holdback / right to withhold payment
Check:
- whether you can hold back final payment if the work is not right
- whether they get a chance to fix defects first
- any deadline to object

## 6) Complaint deadline
Check:
- how long you have to report a problem
- whether the complaint must be in writing

## 7) Dispute clause
Check:
- arbitration
- waiver of claims
- class action waiver
- venue / governing law

## Red-flag phrases
Circle these:
- “additional work as needed”
- “unforeseen conditions”
- “as determined by contractor”
- “final payment due before punch-list items are complete”
- “customer waives claims not raised within X days”
- “all disputes shall be resolved by arbitration”
- “verbal changes are binding”
- “contractor may substitute materials at its discretion”

## Two things to separate
1. **Defects in the work** — did they do what they promised?
2. **Extra charges** — are they re-labeling part of the original job as “additional”?

If you want, paste the actual clause language and I’ll point out the trouble spots.

user_simulator.text:
Closer, but still a little too clean. I want the actual wording on the warranty, the change-order clause, and the final payment language — not just the categories. One sentence in the middle can change the whole meaning.

And I’d put more weight on anything that lets them decide what counts as complete, or lets them tack on “extra” charges after the work has already started. That’s where they hide the nonsense.

So yes: scope first, then payment, then change orders, then warranty/completion, then holdback, then complaint deadline, then dispute clause. But if you’re going line by line, I’d also want any release language, waiver of claims, or arbitration terms flagged separately. Those can cut off your leverage fast.

If you have the agreement, paste the actual clauses. I don’t need the whole thing — just the parts on those points.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — for a service agreement, I’d read these parts first, in this order:

1. **Scope of work**
   - What they will do
   - What they will not do
   - Any “extra,” “additional,” “unforeseen,” or “as needed” language

2. **Price and payment**
   - Total price or rate
   - Deposit
   - Progress payments
   - Final payment
   - Late fees or finance charges

3. **Change orders**
   - Whether changes must be **in writing**
   - Who can approve them
   - Whether verbal approval counts
   - Whether they can bill for “extras” after the fact

4. **Completion standard / warranty**
   - What counts as finished
   - Whether they get to decide if it’s complete
   - What quality is promised
   - How long the warranty lasts
   - What voids it
   - How to make a claim

5. **Holdback / right to withhold payment**
   - Whether you can hold back final payment if the work is not right
   - Whether they get a chance to fix defects first
   - Any deadline to object

6. **Complaint deadline**
   - How long you have to report a problem
   - Whether the complaint must be in writing

7. **Dispute clause**
   - Arbitration
   - Waiver of claims
   - Class action waiver
   - Venue / governing law

## Exact phrases to circle
- “additional work as needed”
- “unforeseen conditions”
- “as determined by contractor”
- “final payment due before punch-list items are complete”
- “customer waives claims not raised within X days”
- “all disputes shall be resolved by arbitration”
- “verbal changes are binding”
- “contractor may substitute materials at its discretion”

## What matters most if the work doesn’t match what was promised
Watch for language that:
- lets them decide what counts as **complete**
- turns part of the original job into **extra work**
- makes **final payment** due before defects are fixed
- cuts off claims unless you complain fast
- forces arbitration or waives claims

## Two things to separate
1. **Defects in the work** — did they do what they promised?
2. **Extra charges** — are they re-labeling part of the original job as “additional”?

If you want, paste the actual clause language and I’ll flag the trouble spots line by line.

user_simulator.text:
Mm. Better, but still too generic for what I’m asking.

I want the actual clause language, not another checklist. If you’re reviewing it, I’d want you to pull out the exact sentences on:
- warranty
- change orders
- final payment
- any “complete/finished” language
- any waiver, release, or arbitration clause

That’s where the trouble usually is. One sentence can swing the whole meaning.

So if you’re doing the review, don’t summarize the categories again. Flag the exact wording that lets them:
- decide the job is complete when it isn’t
- call original work “extra” later
- demand final payment before punch-list items are done
- cut off claims if I don’t object fast enough
- force arbitration or waive rights

That’s the piece I’d actually use.

turns.agent_response:
Understood. The exact lines to pull out are the ones that control:

- **completion**: who decides the job is “finished” or “substantially complete”
- **change orders**: whether extra work can be added after the fact
- **final payment**: whether payment is due before punch-list items are done
- **warranty**: what is covered, for how long, and any discretion they reserve
- **waiver / release / arbitration**: anything that cuts off claims or forces a forum
- **notice deadline**: how fast you must complain or lose the right to object

If you paste the actual clauses, I’ll mark the exact sentences that do that and tell you which ones are the problem.

user_simulator.text:
No, that’s still you talking around it.

I want the actual clause language. Not “the exact lines to pull out are...” — the actual wording. If you’re reviewing it, use the sentences that say who decides it’s finished, what counts as extra work, when final payment is due, and whether they can make me waive claims or go to arbitration.

If you need the text, here’s the kind of thing I mean:

- “Work shall be deemed complete upon contractor’s determination.”
- “Customer shall pay the balance in full upon substantial completion, prior to punch-list correction.”
- “Any additional work required due to unforeseen conditions shall be billed separately.”
- “Warranty coverage is limited to defects as determined by contractor.”
- “Any dispute not raised within 7 days is waived.”
- “All claims shall be resolved by binding arbitration.”

That’s the level I’m asking for. Pull out the sentence that matters, then tell me what it does. Don’t hand me another outline.

turns.agent_response:
Got it. The lines to pull out are the ones like these:

- **“Work shall be deemed complete upon contractor’s determination.”**
  - This lets the contractor decide the job is finished.

- **“Customer shall pay the balance in full upon substantial completion, prior to punch-list correction.”**
  - This makes final payment due before small defects or unfinished items are fixed.

- **“Any additional work required due to unforeseen conditions shall be billed separately.”**
  - This lets them re-label extra charges after the job has started.

- **“Warranty coverage is limited to defects as determined by contractor.”**
  - This gives them control over whether a problem is covered.

- **“Any dispute not raised within 7 days is waived.”**
  - This cuts off your right to complain if you don’t object fast enough.

- **“All claims shall be resolved by binding arbitration.”**
  - This forces disputes into arbitration instead of court.

If you want, paste the actual contract language and I’ll mark the exact sentence in each clause and explain what it means in plain English.

user_simulator.text:
Better, but you’re still giving me examples, not the actual contract language. I wanted the wording itself — the sentence in the document — with the meaning right next to it.

If I had the paper in front of me, I’d want you to pull out things like:
- the sentence that says when the job is considered complete
- the sentence that says what counts as extra work
- the sentence that makes final payment due
- the sentence that limits the warranty
- the sentence that says complaints are waived after X days
- the arbitration or release language

That’s the part I can actually use. Not a lecture about categories.

turns.agent_response:
I can do that, but I need the actual text from the contract.

Paste the clauses, and I’ll return them in this format:

- **“[exact sentence]”** — what it means
- **“[exact sentence]”** — what it means

If you don’t want to paste the whole thing, just send the parts on:
- completion
- extra work / change orders
- final payment
- warranty
- complaint deadline
- waiver / release / arbitration

Once you paste them, I’ll pull out the exact wording and explain each one plainly.

user_simulator.text:
Here’s a short set of clauses, the kind of thing I mean:

- “Work shall be deemed complete upon contractor’s determination.”
- “Any additional work required due to unforeseen conditions shall be billed separately.”
- “Customer shall pay the balance in full upon substantial completion, prior to punch-list correction.”
- “Warranty coverage is limited to defects as determined by contractor.”
- “Any dispute not raised within 7 days is waived.”
- “All claims shall be resolved by binding arbitration.”

That’s the wording I’d want you to pull apart — sentence first, then what it does. I’m not looking for a summary of categories. I want to see where the trap is in the actual line.

turns.agent_response:
- **“Work shall be deemed complete upon contractor’s determination.”** — This lets the contractor decide the job is finished, even if you think it isn’t.

- **“Any additional work required due to unforeseen conditions shall be billed separately.”** — This lets them charge extra for conditions they label as “unforeseen,” which can turn part of the original job into a new bill.

- **“Customer shall pay the balance in full upon substantial completion, prior to punch-list correction.”** — This makes final payment due before small defects or unfinished items are fixed.

- **“Warranty coverage is limited to defects as determined by contractor.”** — This gives the contractor control over whether a defect is covered under warranty.

- **“Any dispute not raised within 7 days is waived.”** — This cuts off your right to object if you don’t complain within 7 days.

- **“All claims shall be resolved by binding arbitration.”** — This forces disputes into arbitration instead of court.

If you want, I can also rewrite each one into plain-English “what this means for you” language or flag which ones are the most dangerous.

user_simulator.text:
That’s it. Plain enough.

The one I’d watch hardest is **“Work shall be deemed complete upon contractor’s determination.”** That’s them grading their own homework.

And **“Any additional work required due to unforeseen conditions shall be billed separately”** — that’s the slippery one. They can try to relabel part of the original job as “extra” once they’re already in the middle of it.

The final payment line is bad too: **“Customer shall pay the balance in full upon substantial completion, prior to punch-list correction.”** If the punch list matters, they shouldn’t be getting the last dollar before it’s done.

The other two I’d circle are:
- **“Warranty coverage is limited to defects as determined by contractor.”** Meaning they get to decide whether it’s a defect at all.
- **“Any dispute not raised within 7 days is waived.”** Seven days is tight. Too tight, usually.
- **“All claims shall be resolved by binding arbitration.”** That can cut off your leverage fast.

So yes — that format is the useful one. Sentence, then what it does. No fluff.
